speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 4 of the Mr. Thomas Simon interview. So your father decided to, to leave Hungary. He wanted to get out of Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he wanted to get-- obviously, he wanted to get out of Hungary. And his plan was that my brother and I go ahead into Israel. And then they'll follow. And now, of course, we couldn't legally leave Hungary at that point. So we, we were actually assisted by the Zionist organization in Hungary and left-- we escaped the country. And we escaped through Czechoslovakia, and then Austria into Italy, and then from Italy to Israel. That was in-- we left in April and arrived, I think, May 16, 1949. So Israel was only about a year old at that point. It wasn't the kind of country that it is today. We were taken to a, a kibbutz. Actually, it was a-- the Youth-Aliyah program. And we stayed about two years in Kvutzat Matsuva, which is at the-- in the-- in the Western Galilee, north of Nahariya. After which-- at that point, my father and mother came, immigrated to Israel as well. They, they actually managed to get exit visas by 1951. And we settled in Jerusalem. My brother was called to the army. I stayed behind because we were allowed to-- one, one son was allowed to stay with the family, whilst the other was in the army. I was actually called to the army only-- I think it was the end of 1952. I served my two-and-a-half years in the Israeli Army. And then the rest of the time, I worked as a civil servant actually in the Ministry of Education in Jerusalem. And 1956, both my brother and I left for England. We stayed in England a couple of years-- I-- about two-and-a-half years. I actually enrolled in the University of London and did some-- a couple of years there. And eventually, we ended up in Canada as a landed immigrants, both my-- my brother actually ahead of me, about six months ahead of me. And then I joined him. In '58, I landed in-- as a landed immigrant in Canada. At that point, I was already enrolled at McGill to continue my studies and, and lived in Montreal. My brother lived in Toronto. And I met my wife, my future wife in Montreal. We got married in 1960. And then we moved to-- I got a job in Ottawa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We moved to Ottawa, I think, in '61-- stayed there for about 10 years. Then we moved back to Montreal for a number of years. And then, finally, in 1976, we arrived in Toronto. And we lived in Toronto ever since. I have three children. I'm sorry, have three children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My wife's name is [? Lisa. ?] She was born in Montreal. All my children were born Montreal-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] David, Daniel, and Tamar. And Tamar just got married last year, last October, to a very nice young man by the name of Andrew Poslence. And David and Danny are still single. And we have no grandchildren at this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I take you back for just a moment to when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to when you left Pecs to go to Israel. Now, what was the name of the Zionist group that took you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I honestly don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It must-- I assume it was affiliated with Mapai at the time, which is Labor Party now, because the kibbutz we ended up in was a-- was a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which is a-- which, I believe, is a Mapai organization. But I'm not absolutely certain whether, in fact, that Zionist organization was Mapai or Mapai-oriented. I, I wasn't-- at that point, I wasn't a great Zionist. I mean, in Pecs, after the war, I, I had no special feelings for Israel at that point. I sort of got-- gravitated towards the Zionist organization because that's where all the girls were. I mean, [LAUGHS] as simple as that, and-- the Jewish girls. And so we sort of became active in the organization. But I had no special affinity in those days to-- for, for Israel. And I, I grew fond of the country after I arrived. And I became a, a very dedicated Zionist. I-- in fact, I had some misgivings about leaving Israel for, for England. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the time, it was more at the urging of my father that we left the-- Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now the trip from Pecs to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was quite an arduous-- it was a, a circuitous trip. As I mentioned, we, we-- there was no legal way for us to, to leave. And we had to-- we had to basically trust the Zionist organization. They were quite secretive and justly so. They would not tell us how we're going to get out of the country. We were simply, at some point, called to, to Budapest from Pecs. And the next day, we were put in a taxi. And, and we had no idea. There was another family in the taxi, incidentally. We had no idea who they were. And we didn't know where the driver is going to take us. And we had no idea whether we're going to go through the Austrian border, the Czech border, or the Romanian border, or the Yugoslav border. It turned out we, we, we were going towards Czechoslovakia. And we crossed the border, we-- at night, walking through fields and, and with a guide, who was obviously paid off. And then eventually, we were-- ended up in Czechoslovakia. We were hiding in there and got on a train. We didn't speak any Czech. And so we pretended to, to be asleep when the conductor came to check the tickets. It, it it was quite interesting, in a way. It was all, all exciting. And eventually, we ended up in Vienna and stayed in Vienna for a few weeks. And in Vienna, we were actually in the American sector, I believe. But being a, a, a, a kid of 16, I was-- we were-- we didn't quite appreciate all the dangers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So occasionally, we did go over to the Russian zone. [LAUGHS] First of all, the concerts were there. And the opera was there. And, and, and so we, we didn't think much of it. And then we went over to the Russian zone. We had no problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we-- it, it was amazing. Some of it was, was, was-- must have been lots of money paid to, to even to the Russians. I recall when, eventually-- at-- by the time we got to Vienna, there was a lot of refugees from Hungary, hundreds, literally hundreds, if not thousands. And we were actually organized and put into buses and-- towards Salzburg. But to go from Vienna to Salzburg, somehow we had to cross the, the Russian zone. Because Austria was divided into four zones in those days. And I recall, at some point, the buses got off the road and actually went through the fields. And some Russian soldiers came on board. And all they did was they count heads. Because they must have been paid by the head. And they counted all the-- all the people on board and let us go through. It was fantastically organized. And by the time we got to Salzburg, it was okay. It was American zone again. And from then, we went down by train to Italy. And I think we, we boarded the-- an Israeli boat. I think it was Israeli boat in Bari, in southern Italy, and ended up in, in Israel--

speaker: interviewer
text: If we can leap--

speaker: interviewee
text: Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we can leap forward a bit now, why, why did you go to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, I think my father-- it was my-- at the urging of my father. And I, you know, I didn't disagree with him. But I wasn't all that enthusiastic. He felt that we should-- that the prospects in Israel weren't very good if you wanted to-- I mean, I tried to-- we weren't well off then. And, and I tried to-- we tried to better ourselves. And, and I didn't think I could go to university in, in-- and we, we really wouldn't have the money to go to university in, in, in Israel. And so we tried. We went on a visitor's visa to England. And we stayed there and-- on a student visa. I, I, I, as I said, enrolled the University of London. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but our-- I, I, I know my father's real interest was to-- for us to get either to Canada or Australia. It was a-- it was a toss-up. [LAUGHS] It-- really, we applied to both places. And the visa came through first in Canada. If it had come through Australia-- on the Australian visa first, I would be in Sydney today or somewhere, or, or more in Fremantle. And, and it just so happened the Canadian visa came through first. So we ended up in, in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you do today in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm-- basically right now, I'm, I'm kind of retired from my, my job. I'm trying to set up a consultancy. I'm a consultant on consumer product testing and, and on consumer products in general. But I worked many years in that field. And I, I got a degree from McGill. But it's a science degree. And I also got a business degree from York, an MBA. And so I, I think I'll, I'll be able to do some-- I'm, I'm 62 now. So I haven't got a very great future ahead of me. But, but, but I'm-- I think I'm too young to retire and-- completely. So I'm trying to set up something for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: If there was one thing that you'd like to tell your future grandchildren or the world about your experience in, in the Holocaust as a whole, what would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a difficult one. [SIGHS] There are a number of things that come to mind. For one thing, I, I resent very much [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- some of the people who would say they stayed alive in the Holocaust because they had a will to live. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In my experience, in my opinion-- and, and, and I'm, I'm quite-- I, I feel very strongly about that-- 90% [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was, was pure luck, then maybe 10% was willpower. Now, that's not something that you want, want to tell your grandchildren necessarily, that to be successful, you have to be lucky-- and only lucky. But this is something that I, I, I feel very strongly about. And I, I resent, as I say, people who, who might say that they stayed alive because they had the willpower to stay alive. Another thing that I would-- I would strongly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] urge, I suppose, my grandchildren, is not to judge, not to generalize people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even in, in, in the worst hellhole like Auschwitz, there were some acts of kindness on the part of the people who were supposedly, you know-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Perpetrating--

speaker: interviewee
text: --cruel and the perpetrators of the crime. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Don't be intolerant. Try and be as tolerant as you possibly can towards any kind of race and nationality, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which doesn't mean that you should allow people to, to push you around. But try and, and, and judge each individual on his or her merits-- merit and, and don't-- please don't generalize, not nations, not groups of people, not professions, not any kind of group. Because in each group-- and that's, that's-- that seems to be a fact of life-- there are good people, and there are bad people. There are decent people. And there are wicked people. And you cannot group them all together. And you have to be tolerant towards any kind of race. I, I was in-- I happened to be-- I was in Berlin some years ago in a-- I was representing, actually, Canada in a scientific meeting in a organization, standards organization. And the meeting was in Berlin. That was the first and the only time I ever was in Berlin. And I, I must admit, when I-- and it was back-- it was sometime in the '80s. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The, the communists were still in East Berlin. It was still two, two, two cities. And we were in West Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I couldn't help, when I saw a man-- I was walking on the street. And I saw a man who was in his 70s and 80s. I couldn't help thinking, what was this guy doing-- what was this man doing during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: But when it came to the younger generation, I had absolutely no problem. I, I had no animosity. I had no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I didn't feel any hostility towards them. But I did have a certain kind of hesitancy when I was dealing with the older generation, people in their 70s and 80s. And, and that's something I can't help. But I try to be as tolerant and, and as understanding of the-- certainly, of the younger generation as, as I possibly can.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for allowing us to interview you, Mr. Simon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is a photograph of my extended family on my late mother's side. It must've been taken in probably 1938 or '39. I-- I'm in the middle there between my mother and my grandmother, sitting. And my brother Peter is on my mother's other side. What I'd like to point out about this photo is that in the background, there are five men who were actually-- all of the-- they were taken to, to the labor brigade before we were deported. So they were not with us during the deportation. But all the other people, individuals, in that picture, were in the same cattle car with me and my mother and brother and sister. And besides, there were-- actually, since that picture's been taken, two other children were born to some of the couples. And I noticed-- basically, as I understand it, there's 18, as I recall, 18 people of my family, 16 of whom are-- or 15 or 16 in, in this picture-- were taken to Auschwitz in the same cattle car. And only my brother and I survived. All the others are, are-- they perished, except for the five men in the back, one of them being my father, who survived, and two or three of the other men, who survived. This photograph was taken in March 1945, a few days after my brother and I returned from Auschwitz to Pecs. It was actually taken by a professional photographer. We were-- by that time, we had some decent clothes on. But we were dressed up in these rags because this is how we arrived back from Auschwitz. I-- I'm the one, as we look at the picture, I'm on the left. And my brother Peter is on the right. Those bags that we carry were actually made by us. They were designed to carry bread or other food that the-- during our march from Auschwitz and our transportation by-- either by, by train or by truck through the-- Poland then Czechoslovakia into Hungary. This picture has been, actually-- been published by magazine. And it, it appears in many publications on the Holocaust. I understand it's-- also appears in the Jewish community offices in Pecs and in many other places. It's fairly well known. This photograph was taken in 1947, I think, just prior to my father remarrying. As we face the photograph, I'm on the right. And my brother, Peter, is on the left. And, of course, my father is in the center. This is the surviving half of the family. I should mention that my father was actually-- he-- his name is Somogyi. But he was born [NON-ENGLISH]. My grandfather, his father, changed his name from [NON-ENGLISH] to Somogyi back at the beginning of the century. And, and, of course, that was the name that I was born with, Somogyi. And then only when I came to Canada-- after I lived in Canada for a number of years, I decided to change my name to Simon. This photograph was taken last October, that is, October 16, 1994. I know the exact date because that's the date of my daughter's wedding. And this is obviously one of those wedding pictures. At the center of the picture is my daughter, Tamar, and her husband, Andrew Poslence. Tamar is now a full-fledged-- almost full-fledged lawyer. She's articling at this point. And she'll be called to the bar next February. Andrew is-- has got a master's degree in international relations from Johns Hopkins. And they both work for the-- at this point, for the Ontario government. Now, if we go from left to right as we face the picture, Daniel is my middle son. He is actually right now in Seattle, Washington. He works for-- he's got a PhD in computer science. And he works for Microsoft. And next one is myself, and on the other side, my wife, [? Lisa. ?] She, she graduated from McGill with a bachelor of library science degree, Bachelor of Arts in library science degree. And on the far right is David, my oldest-- my older son. And he's got a bachelor of business administration from York. And he works for a, a customs brokerage.
